era_context: Late Akkadian period; Egyptian Sixth Dynasty in decline; Indus Valley Civilization at mature phase. The broader context is one of environmental stress (paleoclimate evidence suggests increased aridity in the 22nd century BCE) and simultaneous decline of major civilizations in Mesopotamia and Egypt.

events.title: Akkadian Empire: advanced state of political fragmentation
events.description: The Akkadian Empire is in an advanced state of fragmentation. Central authority from Akkad is minimal. Gutian pressure from the Zagros continues. Sumerian city-states are increasingly independent. This period represents the final decades of Akkadian hegemony before the Gutian occupation becomes dominant (traditionally dated c. 2154 BCE).
events.certainty_note: The collapse of Akkadian authority is well-attested, but precise dating has a margin of error of ±10–20 years.

events.title: Egyptian Sixth Dynasty: nomarch autonomy and decline of central authority
events.description: The Sixth Dynasty pharaohs are increasingly powerless. Provincial governors (nomarchs) rule their regions independently. The transition from the Old Kingdom to the First Intermediate Period is well underway. Monumental building projects have ceased. Local elite culture dominates.
events.certainty_note: The political fragmentation is well-attested. Precise dating remains uncertain (±50–100 years).

events.title: Indus Valley Civilization: mature urban phase; trade networks active
events.description: The Indus Valley Civilization continues in its mature phase with no evidence of disruption. Urban centres remain fully developed and integrated. Trade networks continue to function. The civilization shows stability and continuity during this period when other major civilizations are experiencing collapse.
events.certainty_note: The continuity of the Indus Valley Civilization is well-established through archaeology.
cross_references: Contemporary with Akkadian and Egyptian collapses but shows no parallel decline

disconfirming_evidence: No specific disconfirming evidence identified for events listed. The broader climate-collapse narrative remains contested.
historiographic_note: This year falls in a period of sparse documentation. Mesopotamian and Egyptian records are the primary sources. The Indus Valley Civilization is known almost entirely through archaeology. The absence of written records from most regions means that major developments outside the Near East are entirely unknown.

graph_edges.note: Akkadian and Egyptian collapses occur in the same era
Indus Valley Civilization shows stability while Akkad collapses
